Hallucinations.text: The FBI charged a Philadelphia woman, Keonna Thomas, with attempting to travel overseas to fight for ISIS. She is one of three women arrested this week on terror charges. According to an FBI complaint, Thomas sent numerous social media messages dating back to August 2013, expressing her desire to join ISIS and become a martyr. The messages included "If we truly knew the realities... we all would be rushing to join our brothers in the front lines pray ALLAH accept us as shuhada [martyrs]" and "When you're a mujahid [violent jihadi fighter] your death becomes a wedding." The FBI also stated that Thomas purchased an electronic visa to Turkey on March 23, which is known as the easiest place to enter Syria and join ISIS. An ISIS manual advises recruits to buy round-trip tickets to vacation spots such as Spain and then purchase tickets for their real destination once they arrive overseas. Thomas was charged with knowingly attempting to provide material support and resources to a designated foreign terrorist organization and could face up to 15 years in prison.

Blue Bell ice cream has temporarily shut down one of its manufacturing plants due to the discovery of listeria contamination in a serving of ice cream originating from that plant. Public health officials have warned consumers not to eat any Blue Bell-branded products made at the Broken Arrow, Oklahoma, plant, including 3-ounce servings with codes O, P, Q, R, S, or T behind the coding date. The recall does not affect other Blue Bell ice cream, including other 3-ounce servings not made at the plant

Blue Bell has recalled other products and is shutting down the Broken Arrow facility "out of an abundance of caution" to search for a possible cause of contamination. The CDC advises individuals and institutions to check their freezers for the recalled products and throw them away. This is the first product recall in Blue Bell Creameries' 108-year history. Listeriosis is a serious infection caused by eating food contaminated with listeria, and primarily affects the elderly, pregnant women, newborns, and people with weakened immune systems. Symptoms of a listeria infection include fever and muscle aches, sometimes associated with diarrhea or other gastrointestinal symptoms.

According to the Columbia Journalism School professors' outside review, the failure of Rolling Stone's article about a purported gang rape at the University of Virginia resulted in an institutional failure in the magazine's editorial process. The review found that the failure encompassed reporting, editing, editorial supervision, and fact-checking. The magazine's managing editor, Will Dana, apologized to readers and those affected by the article.

Mary Kay Letourneau and her husband, Vili Fualaau, are still happily together despite her jail time for their sexual relationship. They will celebrate their 10th wedding anniversary on May 20. During an exclusive interview with Barbara Walters on ABC's "20/20," Letourneau will discuss what makes their marriage work, despite their age difference, and her plans to teach again. Fualaau will also share his experiences with alcoholism, depression, and why he believes the system failed him while he was still a minor.

Kanye West has settled a lawsuit with a paparazzi photographer he assaulted, Daniel Ramos, after the hip-hop star pleaded no contest to a misdemeanor count of battery. The case had been set for trial next week, but the parties settled the case to the satisfaction of both parties. As part of the settlement, West issued an apology to Ramos. The incident occurred in July 2013 at Los Angeles International Airport, where West attempted to wrestle Ramos' camera from him after the photographer asked him questions. The case sent an important message that celebrities are not above the law and have no right to physically attack someone simply because they were asked a question.

B.B. King, the legendary blues musician, was hospitalized for dehydration due to his Type II diabetes. However, his daughter, Claudette King, told the Los Angeles Times that he is now feeling much better and is set to leave the hospital today. According to Angela Moore, a publicist for Claudette King, B.B. King is back home resting and spending time with his grandchildren
King remains grateful for the concerns expressed by his fans and is determined to continue his music career

King has used various models of Gibson guitars over the years, including his signature model Lucille, which was officially dropped by Gibson in the 1980s. Some of his notable hits include "The Thrill Is Gone," "There Must be a Better World Somewhere," and "When Love Comes to Town," a collaboration with U2. Last year, King canceled the remainder of his tour due to dehydration and exhaustion after a show in Chicago.

Getting caught napping on the job in the cargo hold of a plane is never a good idea. Alaska Airlines Flight 448 was on its way to Los Angeles from Seattle-Tacoma International Airport when the pilot reported unusual banging sounds coming from the cargo hold. The banging turned out to be a ramp agent from Menzies Aviation who had fallen asleep. The agent was taken to a hospital as a precaution but was later released. The plane was delayed for about 14 minutes before landing safely in Los Angeles
All ramp employees undergo full criminal background checks before being hired and have security badges. The incident is being investigated by Alaska Airlines.

Emy Afalava, a loyal American and decorated veteran, was born in American Samoa, a U.S. territory since 1900. Despite being subject to American law his whole life, Afalava has been denied the right to vote because the federal government insists that he is no citizen. This injustice clouds the recent celebration of the 115th anniversary of American Samoa's decision to join the United States. Afalava and other American Samoans are now seeking to right this wrong in a federal lawsuit before the D.C. Circuit. A decision could come any day.
The Fourteenth Amendment declares that "All persons born ... in the United States, and subject to the jurisdiction thereof, are citizens of the United States."

In the past, the Court has justified this discrimination by citing racial and social concerns. For example, in the Gonzalez case, the Court declined to decide whether or not a Puerto Rican woman was a citizen, citing fears that islanders were "savages" and racially unfit for citizenship.

The Pope faced a difficult decision when he prepared to commemorate the 100th anniversary of the Armenian genocide. He could have used the word "genocide" directly, but he chose to quote his predecessor, John Paul II, instead. This decision was influenced by the Vatican's desire for continuity and the need to maintain good relations with Turkey, a key ally in the fight against ISIS. However, Turkey was not pleased with the Pope's statement and recalled its ambassador for consultations. The Pope's willingness to engage in geopolitics and his blunt tone in addressing moral issues set him apart from his predecessors. He has shown a willingness to take bold action, such as brokering a detente between the US and Cuba, and inviting Israeli and Palestinian leaders to the Vatican. However, he has also shown a willingness to use force to protect Christians from ISIS and to speak out against injustice, as seen in his comments on the Mexicanization of Argentina and the insulting of religion. On Monday, the Pope encouraged his priests to speak frankly and boldly, without fear of reprisal.

The 2015 ACM Awards were bigger than ever, celebrating 50 years of the prestigious country music event. The hosts, Blake Shelton and Luke Bryan, kicked off the night with an energetic opening performance, setting the tone for the rest of the show

Eric Church and Keith Urban provided a standout performance with their joint kickoff, showcasing their powerful vocals. Reba McEntire proved why she's a country music legend with her flawless performance, and Taylor Swift's appearance in the audience was a highlight of the night. Garth Brooks' emotional tribute to the U.S. military was a poignant moment, and Christina Aguilera's surprise appearance with Rascal Flatts was a unexpected but enjoyable surprise. Miranda Lambert dominated the awards, and Jason Aldean brought his powerful vocals to the stage with a medley of hits.

, not everything was perfect, with some performers struggling with their vocals and some of the milestone awards feeling rushed. Additionally, Steven Tyler's facial hair was criticized on social media, and some country fans took to Twitter to criticize Taylor Swift for attending the awards after transitioning to pop music.

el in Hyde Park over his immigration policy. Zylinski, a Polish Prince, is fed up with Farage's discrimination against Poles living in Britain. He laid down a verbal gauntlet, challenging Farage to a duel in the traditional manner of an 18th century Polish aristocrat and English gentleman
Farage declined the challenge, stating that he does not have a sword and does not intend to cross swords with the prince

Seven people, including Illinois State University associate men's basketball coach Torrey Ward and deputy athletic director Aaron Leetch, died in a plane crash while returning from the NCAA tournament final. The small plane went down overnight Monday about 2 miles east of the Central Illinois Regional Airport in Bloomington, McLean County Sheriff's Office Sgt. Bill Tate said. The National Transportation Safety Board tweeted that the plane was a Cessna 414. Ward and Leetch were well-respected and much-loved colleagues in the athletics department, according to a statement from Illinois State University President Larry Dietz. The university will support their families in any way possible.

Sofia Vergara is involved in a legal battle with her ex-fiance, Nick Loeb, over two frozen embryos they created together. Loeb is suing Vergara to prevent her from destroying the embryos, which he wants to implant in a surrogate and bring to term. The lawsuit cites the couple's prior agreement to use the embryos for reproduction, and Loeb believes that life begins at fertilization. Vergara's representatives have declined to comment on the matter. The case raises questions about who has the right to embryos and the legal implications of disagreements over their use.

a 65-year-old woman from Luxor, Egypt, has been dressing as a man for 43 years to avoid oppression and earn a living in a conservative Muslim society. Despite facing discrimination and skepticism, Dahou has worked hard as a brick maker and saved enough money to buy a shoeshine kit, which she now uses to support her daughter and grandchildren.

Dahou's story gained attention after her interview on Egyptian television network CBC, where she spoke about her decision to dress as a man and her determination to protect her daughter and grandchildren from poverty and neglect. The governor of Luxor province was moved by her story and offered her a kiosk and a cash advance, which Dahou accepted, but only on the condition that she would wear her male attire.

Despite the challenges she has faced, Dahou remains proud of her decision to dress as a man and protect her family. She believes that she has earned the respect of her community and the admiration of Egypt's President, Abdel Fattah el-Sisi, who crowned her as one of Egypt's ideal mothers in an official ceremony last month.

Aaron Hernandez, a former New England Patriots tight end, was convicted of first-degree murder

sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole. Despite his $40 million NFL career and seemingly perfect life, Hernandez was found guilty of killing Odin Lloyd in 2013. The trial was notable for Hernandez's lack of remorse or shame, as he walked into court with an air of bravado and even winked at his fiancee during the proceedings. However, the jury carefully considered the damning mountain of circumstantial evidence against Hernandez and reached the right decision. The case is a reminder that even seemingly perfect individuals can hide dark secrets and that the NFL does a good job vetting its players. Ultimately, the case serves as a cautionary tale about the unpredictability of human behavior and the importance of seeking help when needed.

He was on a 35-foot Pearson sailboat when a storm capsized it and broke his mast. Despite his injuries, including a broken shoulder, he was able to rig a makeshift mast and sail, but made little headway against the currents. He was able to collect fresh water by collecting rainwater and using a bucket to scoop up fish from his laundry. He survived on a diet of fish and water, and his food and water ran out after 66 days. He was finally rescued by the crew of the Houston Express, a 1,000-foot long container ship, who spotted him on a small boat nearby. He was in surprisingly good condition,

The French prosecutor leading the investigation into the crash of Germanwings Flight 9525, Brice Robin, stated that no videos from on board the plane have been recovered. He emphasized that if someone has any such video, they should immediately provide it to the investigators. The two magazines, Bild and Paris Match, reported that they had obtained a cell phone video showing the final seconds of the flight, but did not post it on their websites. The video is said to include cries for help and metallic banging, and the person watching the video described it as a "very disturbing scene." However, an official with France's accident investigation agency, the BEA, stated that they are not aware of any such video. The co-pilot of the flight, Andreas Lubitz, had a history of depression and had battled the condition before he took the controls of the flight. Lufthansa, the parent company of Germanwings, knew of Lubitz's battle with depression and allowed him to continue training and fly despite this knowledge. The recovery of the victims' personal belongings, including more cell phones, is set to begin on Wednesday. Investigators are still working to identify the victims using DNA analysis and are also looking into the possible motive of Lubitz for downing the jet.

Robert Lewis Burns Jr., the original drummer of Lynyrd Skynyrd, died in a car crash on Friday night in Cartersville, Georgia. He was 64 years old. According to a Georgia State Patrol spokesman, Burns was not restrained at the time of the crash, which occurred shortly before midnight and involved no other cars. Burns was part of the band's original lineup, which formed in Jacksonville, Florida in 1965, and was instrumental in helping the band produce rock anthems such as "Sweet Home Alabama" and "Freebird." Burns left the band before its third studio album in 1975, citing exhaustion from touring, and was not involved in the 1977 plane crash that killed three members of the band, including lead singer Ronnie Van Zant. Lynyrd Skynyrd was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2006.

The submarine is in a dry dock, and there is no ammunition on board. The rubber insulation between the submarine's light and pressure hull is on fire, according to Tass, which reported that the fire began during welding work on the submarine at the Zvyozdochka shipyard in northwestern Russia. Zvyozdochka spokesman Yevgeny Gladyshev told Tass that the sub had been undergoing repairs since November 2013 and that nuclear fuel from the sub's reactor had been unloaded. Gladyshev also stated that there are no armaments or chemically active, dangerous substances, fissionable materials on the sub, and that the enterprise's personnel left the premises when the submarine caught fire, with no one injured. The fire does not pose a threat to people or the shipyard, according to Gladyshev.

The California Public Utilities Commission has ordered Pacific Gas & Electric Co. to pay a record $1.6 billion penalty for unsafe operation of its gas transmission system, including the pipeline rupture that killed eight people in San Bruno in September 2010. The penalty amounts to forced spending on improving pipeline safety, with $850 million going towards "gas transmission pipeline safety infrastructure improvements" and $50 million towards "other remedies to enhance pipeline safety." The commission's president, Michael Picker, stated that PG&E failed to uphold the public's trust and that the CPUC failed to keep vigilant, resulting in lives lost and homes destroyed. PG&E's chief executive officer, Tony Earley, apologized for the tragic event and stated that the company is working to become the safest energy company in the United States.

The driver of the Lamborghini, 24-year-old Tavon Watson of Kissimmee, Florida, lost control of the vehicle and was hospitalized with minor injuries. Petty Holdings, which operates the Exotic Driving Experience at Walt Disney World Speedway, released a statement expressing their deepest sympathies to the families involved.

If a complete stranger asked me for $100 or offered me an apple in a parking lot without explanation, I would politely decline and ask for clarification on their request. I would explain that I am not able to provide financial assistance or accept unsolicited gifts, and that I am here to help answer questions and provide information to the best of my ability. I would then ask if there is anything else I can help with.

As for Jia Jiang's 100 Days of Rejection project, I find it fascinating that he was able to turn his fear of rejection into an opportunity for growth and success. By confronting his fear head-on and embracing rejection as a possibility, he was able to overcome his own limitations and achieve his goals. His tips for making rejection work for you are practical and insightful, and I particularly agree with the importance of not running away after a rejection and of asking why to learn from the experience.
It's also interesting to note that rejection is not just a personal problem, but also a common experience that many people face

Over the last year, more than 25,000 people have fought Ebola infections, resulting in over 10,000 deaths. Despite the efforts of healthcare workers and the containment of the virus, dozens of new cases are still reported each week. The World Health Organization (WHO) has confirmed a total of 30 new confirmed cases of Ebola in the week of April 5, which is the lowest weekly total since May 2014. However, there are concerns that unknown chains of transmission could lead to new infections in the coming weeks. The experience of Ebola has revealed real flaws in government systems that are supposed to protect us, and it has changed the lives of those who have survived the virus. Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton filed a lawsuit against the Biden administration on Tuesday, seeking to stop federal agents from cutting razor wire at the border

efforts to ensure security amid record numbers of immigrants coming to the Texas border. The state has repeatedly clashed with the federal government over efforts to curtail illegal crossings at the Texas-Mexico border, with the Department of Justice suing Texas in July to try to force Gov. Greg Abbott to remove buoys meant to block migrants from crossing the U.S.-Mexico border. Abbott then claimed the buoys were the Border Patrol's idea and plans to restart construction on border walls along the Rio Grande. The lawsuit filed by Paxton alleges that Texas has the sovereign right to construct border barriers to prevent the entry of illegal aliens, and that the Biden administration's actions are illegal and pose a threat to public safety and the environment.

To make a blood smear, you will need a clean glass slide, a needle, and a pair of sterilized gloves. First, sterilize the needle with a flame and then prick your finger to obtain a small drop of blood. Place the drop of blood at one end of the slide. Next, take another clean slide and, holding it at an angle of about 45 degrees, touch the blood with one end of the slide so that the blood runs along the edge of the slide by capillary action. Carefully push the slide along the length of the first slide to produce a thin smear of blood. Repeat this process to make two smears, allowing them to air dry before labeling and placing them in the provided slide transport containers. It is important to note that blood can carry diseases that can be transferred from person to person, so it is important to wear rubber gloves if it is unavoidable to come into contact with another person's blood.
